title: Variables & Types
description: Master Rust's type system and variable bindings — knowing when to use let vs let mut, choosing between i32/i64/f64, and handling Option<T> prevents ownership headaches during interviews.

sections.title: let and let mut bindings
sections.explanation: Rust variables are **immutable by default**. Use `let` for immutable bindings and `let mut` when you need to reassign. In interviews, most counters and accumulators need `mut`. The five types you'll use constantly: **i32**, **i64**, **f64**, **String**/**&str**, and **bool**.

exercise.prompt: Declare five variables: `count` (i32, value 42), `big` (i64, value 1000000000000), `ratio` (f64, value 3.14), `name` (&str, value "alice"), and `found` (bool, value true). Print each on its own line.

validation.message: Each variable should print its value on a separate line
Declare each variable with let and its correct type

exercise.hint: Assign the literals directly: count = 42, big = 1_000_000_000_000, ratio = 3.14, name = "alice", found = true.

sections.title: Type casting with as
sections.explanation: In interviews, you often need to convert between types — integer division to floating-point, usize to i32 for indexing, etc. Use the **as** keyword for numeric casts. For string conversions, use **.parse::<T>()** and **.to_string()**. Remember that `as` truncates silently, so be mindful of overflow.

exercise.prompt: Divide i32 `a = 7` by i32 `b = 2` using `as f64` to get a float result. Then parse the string "99" into an i32, and convert 256 to a String with to_string(). Print all three results.

validation.message: Check your casts — the division should produce 3.5, parse gives 99, to_string gives "256"
Use as f64 for the division, parse() for string-to-int, and to_string() for int-to-string

exercise.hint: Use .parse().unwrap() to convert a string to a number. Print the text variable for the last line.

sections.title: Option<T> and handling None
sections.explanation: Rust has no null — instead it uses **Option<T>**, which is either `Some(value)` or `None`. You'll encounter this constantly with HashMap lookups, vector indexing, and tree nodes. Use `match`, `if let`, or `.unwrap_or()` to safely extract values. Never use `.unwrap()` in production, but it's acceptable in interview code for brevity.

exercise.prompt: Create two Option<i32> values: `val` as Some(10) and `empty` as None. Print each using unwrap_or(0). Then use `if let` on val to print "Found: 10" and a `match` on empty to print "No value".

validation.message: unwrap_or(0) gives 10 for Some(10) and 0 for None
Use unwrap_or(0) for defaults, if let for Some extraction, and match arms for exhaustive handling

exercise.hint: Pass 0 to unwrap_or() for both. Print "No value" in the None arm of the match.

sections.title: Shadowing and constants
sections.explanation: Rust lets you **shadow** a variable by redeclaring it with `let`. This is useful for type conversions without needing `mut`. Use `const` for compile-time constants — these must have explicit types. In interviews, define `const MOD: i64 = 1_000_000_007` for modular arithmetic and `i32::MAX` / `i32::MIN` for sentinels.

exercise.prompt: Declare `x` as 5, shadow it to x + 1, then shadow it again to its f64 cast. Define a constant MOD as 1_000_000_007 (i64). Print x, MOD, and i32::MAX.

validation.message: x should be 6.0 (prints as 6), MOD is 1000000007, i32::MAX is 2147483647
Shadow x with let, cast with as f64, define MOD with const

exercise.hint: Cast x as f64. Set MOD = 1_000_000_007. i32::MAX is already defined in std.
